responseSet.title: The Understanding of Dreams or the Machinations of the Night: By Raymond de Becker.  London:  Allen & Unwin.  1968.  Pp. 432.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Understanding of Dreams or the Machinations of the Night

By Raymond de Becker. London: Allen & Unwin. 1968. Pp. 432.

Oscar Wilde somewhere says: 'The public is wonderfully tolerant. It forgives everything except genius.' As one of Freud's public, Raymond de Becker faithfully exemplifies the truth of Wilde's witticism. In this rather over-ambitious undertaking to inform us of the value of dreams 'as motives for action', the author has only two things to offer: an indiscriminate compilation of interesting dream-material from mythology, history and folklore that stays inert in his fervent adulation of its virtue; and an acrid wrangle with Freud for having made of dreams the inner reality humans live from. Mr Becker is not content with stating that 'an unsolved sexual problem lurks in the background of Freud's theories' of wish-fulfilment in dreams. He retails unidentifiable gossip about what led to Jung's break with Freud: 'While we wait for unpublished Correspondence of the two giants of modern psychology to reveal the full details of the reasons for their break with each other, I would like to put forward a fundamental cause, which was told to me in confidence. There came a time when Jung considered that, by continuing to accept the Freudian theories, life lost its meaning and that there was nothing left but to commit suicide. It was Freud's most intelligent disciples who had committed suicide, those who had best assessed the consequences of his theories; Jung considered that, apart from cowardice, only a lack of intelligence and a total failure to realize the logical outcome of Freud's theories could keep anyone from ending their lives. This is supposed to have been the fundamental reason why Jung broke away.' Is this the sort of data Paul Roazen has acclaimed as: 'For what seems gossip to one generation may be the history of tomorrow'? A dreadful prospect indeed!

558

responseSet.documentRefHTML: Loewenstein, R. M. (1970) Heinz Hartmann—1894–1970. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 51:417-419

responseSet.title: Heinz Hartmann—1894–1970
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Heinz Hartmann—1894–1970

Rudolph Loewenstein

Rudolph Loewenstein

Rudolph Loewenstein  

Heinz Hartmann's sudden death of a coronary thrombosis, on 17 May, created an immense feeling of loss in his family, his many friends, colleagues and students, and admirers all over the world. This loss is due not alone to the cessation of an outstanding scientific career, but to the disappearance of an extraordinary personality. His scientific work has been and will continue to be studied by psychoanalysts, psychiatrists, psychologists and other scientists, and it will continue to have an enormous influence on their thinking and work. But this is not the time or the place to speak of Hartmann the scientist. Tonight we are honouring the memory of a colleague, teacher and friend.

Heinz Hartmann was born in Vienna in 1894. His father was a renowned historian and a leader in adult education for the working class, who served for a time as Austrian Ambassador to Germany after World War I. Heinz's mother, artistically gifted, was a sculptress and an accomplished pianist.

Having obtained his medical degree in 1920 at the University of Vienna, Hartmann soon decided to specialize in psychiatry. Except for one year at the Berlin Institute, where he continued the analytic training he had begun in Vienna, he worked at the Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute of Vienna University from 1920 until 1934. Following that period he was teacher and training analyst first at the Vienna Psychoanalytic Institute, then at the Paris Psychoanalytic Institute and, from 1941 on, at the New York Institute. He was editor of the Internationale Zeitschrift für Psychoanalyse from 1932 until 1941. Since its inception in 1945, he was one of the managing editors of The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, which he founded with Anna Freud and Ernst Kris. He served as Medical Director of the Treatment Center of the New York Psychoanalytic Institute from 1948 to 1951, as President of the New York Psychoanalytic Society from 1952 to 1954, as President of the International Psycho-Analytical Association from 1953 to 1959, and was Honorary President of the latter from 1959 until his death.

responseSet.title: Psyche, 23, No. 4, 1969: N. TREURNIET (de Lairessestr. 128, Amsterdam, Netherlands).  Ein abweichender Verlauf des analytischen Prozesses in der Eröffnungsphase (A deviant form of the beginning analytic process)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 23, No. 4, 1969

N. TREURNIET (de Lairessestr. 128, Amsterdam, Netherlands). Ein abweichender Verlauf des analytischen Prozesses in der Eröffnungsphase (A deviant form of the beginning analytic process)

A deviant form of the beginning analytic process is described in the analysis of a 33-year-old businessman, who experienced acute and chronic threats of annihilation throughout his latency years in World War II, due to intensive bombardments of his native city and the constant stress of impending deportation because of his Jewish father. The analysis started with the presentation of material from the oedipal phase, which very soon turned out to be a defence against anxiety and shame concerning conflicts in adolescence, when he had tried to solve his masturbation conflict by actively listening at the door of the parental bedroom while his parents were having intercourse. When this had been worked through in the transference, it became clear that this form of uncommon adolescent behaviour was determined by and a defence against the intensely destructive nature of his masturbation phantasies. This was intimately connected with his war experiences according to the formula: 'if one is left behind by one's parents in war-time, one is going to be destroyed'. The clinical implications of acute and chronic threats of annihilation during childhood are discussed. The effects of this special form of trauma seem to be an exceptionally intense defence formation against aggressive and passive-feminine drive impulses, a great sensitivity to narcissistic injuries and a sexualization of anxiety and tension. This may cause technical problems concerning regression in the service of the ego. Finally, some technical questions concerning adolescent material in an adult analysis are considered.

75

Psychotherapeutische Behandlung von Studenten im Urteil der Therapeuten (Psychotherapy of students in the therapist's view:  phase-specific conflicts during the student years)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 23, Nos. 10–12, 1969

M. L. MOELLER (Psychosomatic Clinic, Students' Service, University of Giessen, Ludwigstr. 76, 63 Giessen, Germany). Psychotherapeutische Behandlung von Studenten im Urteil der Therapeuten (Psychotherapy of students in the therapist's view: phase-specific conflicts during the student years)

The theoretical basis of Moeller's paper is E. H. Erikson's model of psychosocial developmental phases. The term 'social adolescence' is suggested for the delay inherent in the life situation of students whose independent social and family life is often postponed until adulthood. The resulting phasespecific conflicts are delayed adolescent problems, problems of young adult life and problems arising from the terminal phase of social adolescence. The impact of these psychosocial phases on treatment is illustrated by systematically analysed interviews. One decisive release mechanism of student protest is seen in frustrated identity.

521

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 23, Nos. 10–12, 1969

H. U. ZIOLKO (Psychoterapeutic Counselling Service for Students, Dept. of Psychiatry and Neurology, Free University of Berlin, Nussbaumallee 30–38, 1 Berlin 19, Germany). Zur Problematik der Studenten mit verlängertem Studium (Problems of students with prolonged study time)

Observations made in a student counselling centre are analysed with a view to specific personality traits and behaviour anomalies in students with prolonged stay at the university (from 15 to 34 terms). A somewhat higher rate of psychoses and suicidal attempts was found. Specific problems of this group, e.g. guilt feelings at leaving university and entering professional life, are viewed against a background of a group of non-patients with a history of prolonged studies.

521

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 23, Nos. 10–12, 1969

HERMANN ROSKAMP (Meistersinger-Str. 17, 7 Stuttgart-Degerloch, Germany). ber Identitätskonflikte bei im zweiten Weltkrieg geborenen Studenten (Identity conflicts in students born at the end of the second world war)

One of the most frequent complaints for which students seek advice at counselling centres is a crisis occurring towards the end of a prolonged academic education. Symptoms are incapability of working, loss of orientation, and fear of failing in professional life. This crisis is traced back to a childhood experience specific for this generation of students: the return of their defeated and powerless hero-fathers to the hitherto fatherless family. The conflicts arising from that situation are frequently solved by depreciation of the father-figure, and the resulting negative identification then blocks the way to sexual and social identity.

521

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 23, Nos. 10–12, 1969

MANFRED POHLEN (Research Centre for Psychopathology and Psychotherapy, Max-Planck-Gesellschaft, Munich). Die Abhängigkeitsthematik in der Revolte der Studenten (Dependency conflicts in student rebellion)

Group therapy with students suffering from exhaustion and examination fears revealed underlying unresolved oedipal conflicts. The patients' histories revealed the typical fates of children born shortly after World War II. Their defeated, powerless and previously absent fathers evaded authority conflicts by creating the outward appearance of a liberal family atmosphere. In this situation the key is seen not only to the psychodynamics of examination fears but also to the student protest movement. In agreement with Lacan the author believes that the decline of the father-image, by releasing aggression, is one of the most decisive events determining the world-wide student rebellion.

521

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 23, Nos. 10–12, 1969

EUGEN MAHLER (Psychotherapeutic Counselling Centre for Students of the Studentenwerk, 6 Frankfurt-Main, Germany). Psychische Konflikte und Hochschulstruktur (Psychic conflicts and university organization)

Experiences at a psychotherapeutic counselling agency for students—a 'psychic main dressingstation' of the student protest movement—are the basis for the author's attempt to make us aware that certain institutional conditions (the neurosogenic climate at universities), international political controversies (e.g. the May revolt in Paris or the occupation of Czechoslovakia in 1968) as well as collective psychological processes, i.e. essentially objective conditions, are the matrix and precipitating factors of student neuroses. For a long time, psychic casualties in students were not recognized as problems of their society, either by the general public or by those directly concerned.

521

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 23, Nos. 10–12, 1969

D. BECKMANN, H. E. RICHTER & J. W. SCHEER (Psychosomatic Clinic, University of Giessen, Ludwigstr. 76, 63 Giessen, Germany). Kontrolle von Psychotherapieresultaten (Control of psychotherapeutic results)

First, the common methods of controlling the results of psychotherapy are presented and discussed with regard to their reliability (judgement on the basis of external criteria, judgement by others, judgement by the person concerned). From the criticism of psychological medicine's traditional health concept, whose only orientation is the individual patient, his efficiency and his well-being, the demand is derived to develop supplementary social-psychological criteria of mental health. Frequently, pseudo-remissions due to the formation of defence mechanisms are seen which, while making the patient appear less neurotic, weigh heavily upon his family and work colleagues, since the patient tries to involve them in his illness.

522

[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 23, Nos. 10–12, 1969

A. E. MEYER, H. OTTE, H.-J. SEEBERGER, H. SPEIDEL & R. ZENKER (Psychosomatic Clinic, II. Medizin. Universitätsklinik, Hamburg-Eppendorf). Die Inter-Beobachter-bereinstimmung für die psychoanalytische Einordnung von Charakter- und Verhaltensbeschreibungen (Inter-observer accordances on psychoanalytic descriptions of character and behaviour)

For the purpose of developing a multidimensional personality questionnaire on a psychoanalytic basis, a system of categories consisting of 29 (31) specific scales and four (five) superior global scale areas (oral, anal, phallic-oedipal, unspecific for the phase concerned, to be attributed to more than one phase) was set up. Three analysts placed 159 global (excerpted from the literature) personality items of a typical analytical nature and 687 special behaviour items into this system of categories. The following inter-observer accordances were found. (1) Global categories for global items: (a) three analysts in accordance with literature—31%; (b) at least two analysts in accordance with literature—55%; (c) three analysts in accordance with each other—43%; (d) at least two analysts in accordance with each other—89%; (2) Global categories for special behaviour items: (a) three analysts in accordance with each other—40%; (b) at least two analysts in accordance with each other—76%; (3) Specific categories for special behaviour items: (a) three analysts in accordance with each other—26%; (b) at least two analysts in accordance with each other—66%. This degree of accordance seems to constitute a questionable basis for a test construction; it corresponds, however, to the measure of agreement about the formation of diagnoses published in the field of clinical psychiatry.

522

Psyche, 23, Nos. 10–12, 1969

M. PERREZ (A-5061 Salzburg, Jung Ilsenheimstr. 9, Austria). Bedarf die psychoanalytische Theorie eigener Kriterien der Wissenschaftlichkeit? (Does psychoanalytic theory require specific methodological criteria?)

According to Perrez, psychoanalysis is not, by the peculiarities of its subject matter, in need of any specific methodology different from that of the natural sciences. Rosenkötter's argument in favour of a method regarding psychoanalysis as a systematically generalized history (Habermas) is rejected on grounds of criteria for science elaborated by modern positivists.

522

responseSet.abstract:

Psyche, 23, Nos. 10–12, 1969

L. ROSENKÖTTER (Abt. f. Psychotherapie, University of Ulm, 79 Ulm, Schülinstr. 6). Zu den Diskussionsbeiträgen von S. O. Hoffmann und M. Perrez (Concluding remarks to the discussion by S. O. Hoffmann and M. Perrez)

Rösenkotter, in his objections to the contributions of Hoffmann and Perrez, insists that the 'complexity of the observation fields' of psychoanalysis and social sciences cannot be understood by applying investigation procedures using positivistic criteria; instead, the methods of choice should be adapted to the phenomena under study. Attempts to deny scientific dignity to hermeneutic methods are exposed as effects of a societal tendency to suppress the differences between technique and practice.

523

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 23, Nos. 10–12, 1969

W. W. KEMPER (1 Berlin 19, Länder-Allee 31). Synchrone Verzahnung von Interpretation und Agieren (Synchronous dovetailing of interpretation and acting out)

In this critical contribution on the manifestation of parapsychological phenomena in the analytic situation, a detailed report is given of an instance of acting out within the analytic situation which coincided exactly with an analytic interpretation of the very impulse underlying this acting out. This gives the appearance of telepathy. The psychic processes leading to this remarkable coincidence can without difficulty be explained naturalistically.

523

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 23, Nos. 10–12, 1969

E. ROELLENBLECK (8026 Ebenhausen, near Munich, Gerhart-Hauptmann-Weg 10). Peer Gynt als erotischer Typus sui generis (Peer Gynt: an erotic type sui generis

Ibsen's hero, Peer Gynt, is presented as the typical 'perpetual youth'. Wooing is his domain, but he is 'wrecked by success' and shuns lasting ties. He compensates for the loss of the beloved by hallucinating a patiently waiting mother-wife who is unattainable for direct sexual wishes. Metapsychologically speaking, the problem of this kind of personality may be traced back to a pre-oedipal mother fixation on an oral-narcissistic basis. This would explain the incapacity for real object relations.

523

responseSet.title: Psyche, 24, Nos. 1–6, 1970: W. SCHUMACHER (Neuropsychiatric Clinic, University of Giessen, Am Steg 18, 63 Giessen, Germany).  Bemerkungen zur Theorie des Narzissmus (Notes on the theory of narcissism)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 24, Nos. 1–6, 1970

W. SCHUMACHER (Neuropsychiatric Clinic, University of Giessen, Am Steg 18, 63 Giessen, Germany). Bemerkungen zur Theorie des Narzissmus (Notes on the theory of narcissism)

Freud's concept of a primary narcissism seems to offer a way of integrating analytic drive theory and ego psychology. All drive tension starts from a reservoir of primary narcissism and tends to return to it. Ultimately, satisfaction is sought from the self. With the development of ideal-self structures, the ideal objects, internalized on the basis of narcissistic strivings, enter into the core of the self. Discharge regulation is recognized as a tendency to close the gap between (marginal) internalizations of ideal-self and real self, i.e. a tendency to maintain a certain level of self-esteem and a sense of security. The subjective experience of time seems to be based on narcissistic tension. Mature object relations depend upon a well-balanced inner narcissistic field.

523

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, 24, Nos. 1–6, 1970

RENATE STAEWEN-HAAS (63 Giessen, Marburger-Str. 18, Germany). Identifizierung und weibliche Kastrationsangst (Identification and female castration fears)

Starting from Chasseguet-Smirgel's idea of the 'girl's legitimate wish for a penis' and from Grunberger's and Sandler's comments on narcissism, the author states that, with regard to the oedipal processes of identification, the object-representations of the male and female genitals must be differentiated as to their significance as biological-sexual organs and their symbolic aspects respectively. Neurotic processes and (in the girl) castration fears will arise if the boy's or the girl's self-representations become identified with the sexual aspect of the genitals of the opposite sex. On the other hand, identification with the symbolic aspects of the representations of both the paternal and the maternal genitals are decisive for autonomous development and for the efficiency of the ego functions.

523

Psyche, 24, Nos. 1–6, 1970

H. U. ZIOLKO (Psychiatric-Neurological Clinic of the University of Berlin, Nussbaum-Allee 30–38, 1 Berlin 19, West Berlin). Halluzinationen und Neurose (Hallucinations and neurosis)

Whereas the study of hallucinations is generally concerned with psychotic and organic types only, the author deals with hallucinatory phenomena related to psychic conflicts. His experience is derived from 69 neurotic patients. The manifold content of the hallucinations is distinguished from their formal characteristics (sensory field, facilitation through outer circumstances, etc.); their functions are determined according to metapsychological criteria.

523

Psyche, 24, Nos. 1–6, 1970

WALTER J. REIS (Neville House, 552 N. Neville Street, Pittsburgh 13, Pennsylvania, U.S.A.). Formen der freien Assoziation zu Träumen (Forms of free associations to dreams)

The subject of this paper is defined as a question, 'What inferences, if any, could be drawn from

523

systematic differences in free associating to dreams as compared to other material?' The difficulties which a young woman had in free associating to dream material are presented. The process which led to their gradual disappearance is discussed and the history of free associations in analysis is traced to emphasize current viewpoints. It is concluded that the patient's difficulties in associating to dreams represent a specific pattern of ego defence against loss of control in a setting of distrust. The feasibility of searching for other characterological patterns in dealing defensively with dream material is discussed.

524

Hegel und Freud:  die Kritik der 'Phänomenologie' am Begriff der psychologischen Notwendigkeit und ihre anthropologischen Konsequenzen (Hegel and Freud:  critique of the Phenomenology of Mind concerning psychological necessity and its anthropological consequences)
responseSet.abstract:

Psyche, 24, Nos. 1–6, 1970

ULRICH SONNEMANN (Am Jagdweg 1, 8 Munich 90, Germany). Hegel und Freud: die Kritik der 'Phänomenologie' am Begriff der psychologischen Notwendigkeit und ihre anthropologischen Konsequenzen (Hegel and Freud: critique of the Phenomenology of Mind concerning psychological necessity and its anthropological consequences)

Hegel demonstrates the logical doubts which afflict any psychological theory insofar as it attempts to unravel the true interlacing of subject and society with naive concepts of nature and to substitute a solipsistic metaphysic. The author, departing from this demonstration, pleads for an epistemological revision of Freudian theory in the direction of convergence with a material critique of society. The subjective view of the world feeds upon the experience of strangeness and impotence of individuals in regard to collective institutions. Instead of penetrating the subjective view of the world in this way, Freudian theory makes it into a methodological principle ('psychologism'). An escape from these epistemological difficulties appears in the form of a practice aimed against objectified man as a reversible entity.

524

Psyche, 24, Nos. 1–6, 1970

KLAUS LICKINT (Boxbergring 30, 69 Heidelberg, Germany). Die psychische Steuerung physischer Abläufe, insbesondere bei der Konversion (Psychic control of physiological processes, especially of conversion)

Employing the concepts of psychic controlling values, body-ego and body schema, a reformulation of the process of conversion is undertaken and illustrated with brief case histories. Voluntary movement is the prototype of psychic control. The body-ego has mobility and agility at its disposal—psychic vectors which determine the movement of the anatomical limbs. The experience of phantom limbs and conversion symptoms demonstrates the non-identity of body-ego and body schema.

524
